original_abstract: A key challenge in the field of electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction is the design of catalytic materials featuring high product selectivity, stability, and a composition of earth-abundant elements. In this work, we introduce thin films of nanosized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as atomically defined and nanoscopic materials that function as catalysts for the efficient and selective reduction of carbon dioxide to carbon monoxide in aqueous electrolytes. Detailed examination of a cobalt-porphyrin MOF, Al2(OH)2TCPP-Co (TCPP-H2 = 4,4',4″,4‴-(porphyrin-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrabenzoate) revealed a selectivity for CO production in excess of 76% and stability over 7 h with a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical measurements provided insights into the cobalt oxidation state during the course of reaction and showed that the majority of catalytic centers in this MOF are redox-accessible where Co(II) is reduced to Co(I) during catalysis.
humanized_abstract: Electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction to fuels and chemicals requires catalysts that not only have high selectivity but also excellent stability and composition using only abundant elements. Here we demonstrate the use of thin films of nanosized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as catalysts made of atomically and nanoscopically defined composition and demonstrated their use as efficient and selective catalysts for the reduction of CO2 to CO in aqueous solution. A deep analysis of catalytic behavior of a cobalt-based porphyrin-based MOF material, named Al2(OH)2TCPP-Co, where TCPP-H2 corresponds to 4,4',4″,4‴-(porphyrin-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrabenzoate, was performed with a selectivity to CO >76% along with catalytic stability for more than 7 h and with a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. Detailed understanding of the catalytic process in this material was attained through in situ spectroelectrochemical studies showing redox-active sites where Co2+ is reduced to Co+ under catalytic conditions and where a significant fraction of the Co species in the MOF framework are under reducing conditions and hence catalytically active.
